message.content:
Test file: src/cli.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/cli.ts
Passing tests: 46 (0 todo — all CLI tests implemented and passing)
Recent changes: Added per-type damage-count vs policy-coverage check in assertDamagesValid (throws "Too many damages for item type").

This is the final refactor pass for the CLI module. Review the whole src/cli.ts for any final clarity/duplication improvements while keeping all 46 tests green. Read the file first. Also confirm there is a main() that reads stdin, calls runScenario, writes { results } to stdout, and on a thrown error writes to stderr and exits non-zero — if main() is missing or incomplete (it is needed for the CLI entry point per the spec), note it but do NOT add new untested behavior during refactor; just report it.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 46 tests pass (36 in claim-office, 10 in cli). Tests are green.

Now let me proceed through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

I'll evaluate the names in `src/cli.ts`:

- `runScenario` — clearly reveals intent: runs a scenario and returns results. Good.
- `assertKnownItemTypes` — clearly reveals it asserts all item types are known. Good.
- `assertDamagesValid` — clearly reveals it validates damages. Good.
- `KNOWN_ITEM_TYPES` — clear constant name. Good.
- `policyItemTypes`, `damageCounts`, `coverageCount`, `remainingCaps`, `quoteCount` — all reveal intent. Good.

**Naming Decision:** All names already clearly reveal intent. No renaming needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

I'll focus on the `assertDamagesValid` function (the recently changed code) and look at the whole module for duplication.

**`assertDamagesValid` current mass (approx):**
- It builds `policyItemTypes` via `.map`, loops over damages with two conditionals + a `.includes` + Map mutation, then a second loop with `.filter` + conditional.

A notable observation: `policyItemTypes.filter((type) => type === itemType).length` recomputes coverage counts inside the second loop, iterating the policy items repeatedly. Both loops conceptually compute "counts per type." There's conceptual duplication between counting damages (Map) and counting coverage (filter).

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 46 green. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are clear. The second loop's `policyItemTypes.filter(...).length` is slightly less clear than a named coverage-count map, but it's readable.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** There is conceptual duplication — both damage counts and coverage counts are "count occurrences by type." This is a candidate for a small extraction.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unnecessary abstractions.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll extract the "count by type" concept used for both damage and coverage counting. This removes the conceptual duplication (Rule 3) and improves clarity (Rule 2). Let me apply one focused change.

input.command: cat package.json | grep -A2 '"build"' ; ls node_modules/.bin/tsc 2>/dev/null && npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | tail -10

content.content:
node_modules/.bin/tsc
src/cli.ts(75,38): error TS2345: Argument of type 'number | undefined' is not assignable to parameter of type 'number'.
  Type 'undefined' is not assignable to type 'number'.

content.text:
This TS error pre-exists my change (it's at line 75, the `claim` call's `remainingCap` argument, unrelated to my edit). Let me confirm it's pre-existing by checking whether there's a build script and whether this error exists on the original code path. The error is at line 75 (`remainingCap: remainingCaps.get(step.policy)`), which I did not touch.

Let me verify this is pre-existing rather than introduced by my refactor.

input.description: Check for build/typecheck scripts and strict mode

content.text:
There is no `build` or `typecheck` script in package.json — the project's verification gate is `pnpm test` (vitest), which uses esbuild and does not enforce strict type errors. The TS2345 error at line 75 is pre-existing (it's on the untouched `remainingCaps.get(step.policy)` line, where `Map.get` returns `T | undefined` and `claim`'s param is non-optional). My refactor did not introduce it and does not affect it.

My change to line 67 (`coverageCounts.get(itemType) ?? 0`) correctly handles the `undefined` from `Map.get`, so I introduced no new strict errors.

Let me verify the line numbers of my edit are clean by reading the relevant section.
The refactored code is clean. Now let me address the second part of the request: confirming whether `main()` exists.

Confirmed: there is no `main()` in `src/cli.ts` (or anywhere in `src/`). No `process.stdin`, `process.stdout`, `process.stderr`, `process.exit`, or `readFileSync` usage exists. The module only exports `runScenario`.

Per instructions, I will NOT add untested `main()` behavior during refactor — I'm just reporting it. The refactor is complete and all tests are green.

---

## Refactor Phase Report

**File:** `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`

### Step 1 — Naming Evaluation (first priority)
All names already reveal intent: `runScenario`, `assertKnownItemTypes`, `assertDamagesValid`, `KNOWN_ITEM_TYPES`, `coverageCounts`, `damageCounts`. No renames needed.

### Step 2/3 — Simple Design Rules
- **Rule 1 (Tests pass):** 46/46 green before and after.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Improved — coverage is now an explicit `coverageCounts` map instead of an inline `policyItemTypes.filter(...).length` lookup.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication):** The conceptual duplication of "count occurrences by type" (computed two different ways — a `Map` for damages, a repeated `.filter().length` for coverage) was the strongest improvement target. Extracted a single `countByType` helper used for both damage counts and coverage counts.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** The extraction adds one small helper but removes the repeated inline filtering inside the second loop; net complexity is lower and non-redundant.

### Step 4 — Refactoring applied (mandatory attempt satisfied)
Extracted `countByType(types: string[]): Map<string, number>` and rewrote `assertDamagesValid` to:
- build `coverageCounts` once via `countByType`,
- use `coverageCounts.has(...)` for the not-in-policy check (clearer than `.includes` on a separate array),
- reuse `countByType` for `damageCounts`,
- compare against `coverageCounts.get(itemType) ?? 0` instead of recomputing `.filter().length` inside the loop.

### Step 5 — APP Mass (assertDamagesValid + new helper)
- **Before:** two array builds, two loops, an inline `.filter().length` invocation per damage type (an extra iteration nested in the second loop), plus a Map mutation. The nested `.filter` made the second loop effectively O(types × policyItems).
- **After:** the per-iteration `.filter` invocation (mass 2) and its implicit scan (loop mass 5) are gone from the second loop; coverage lookup is now a single Map `get` (invocation mass 2). The shared `countByType` consolidates two counting idioms into one. Net mass is lower and, more importantly, the repeated nested scan is eliminated. Rule 2 clarity was not sacrificed — it improved.

### Step 6 — Tests
`pnpm test` → 46 passed (36 claim-office + 10 cli). Green.

### Note on TypeScript strict error (pre-existing, not introduced)
`npx tsc --noEmit` reports one error at `src/cli.ts:75` — `remainingCaps.get(step.policy)` yields `number | undefined` but `claim`'s `remainingCap` param... actually the param is optional (`claim-office.ts:108`), so the real flagged line is the `remainingCap` argument passed into `claim`. This error is on code I did not touch and pre-exists this refactor. There is no `build`/`typecheck` script in `package.json`; the project's gate is `pnpm test` (vitest/esbuild), which passes. I did not "fix" it because that would be untested behavior change outside this refactor's scope. My own added line (`coverageCounts.get(itemType) ?? 0`) is strict-safe.

### Note on missing `main()` (reported, NOT added)
`src/cli.ts` has **no `main()`** and no stdin/stdout/stderr/exit handling anywhere in `src/`. The module only exports `runScenario`. The spec describes a CLI entry point that reads stdin, calls `runScenario`, writes `{ results }` to stdout, and on a thrown error writes to stderr and exits non-zero — **this is currently missing/incomplete**. Per the refactor constraint, I did not add it (it would be new untested behavior). It should be driven in via the red/green TDD cycle with its own tests.

Proceeding to the next test.
